title: Welcome
text:
Welcome to my Module 10 website for MSDM. I am a Digital Marketing Master’s student at Cal Poly Pomona with a passion for blending creativity, analytics, and audience engagement.


I am pursuing a career in integrated marketing within the sports and entertainment industry, where I hope to combine data‑driven strategy, fan engagement, and creative storytelling to elevate brand experiences.



I bring hands‑on experience across marketing, events, and customer engagement, including:

Event operations at the Rose Bowl Stadium
Aquatics marketing & HR support at the Rose Bowl Aquatics Center
Brand ambassador campaigns for Farmer John and Disney x Brooks
Digital marketing analytics and GA4 reporting
Creative content development and presentation design

I enjoy turning insights into strategy and crafting meaningful experiences for audiences.

title: Dashboard
text:
This page showcases the static dashboard I created in Module W10 using Quarto.
The goal of this dashboard is to communicate insights from my MSDM CEP project using clear visualizations and audience‑friendly storytelling.

title: Essay on Shiny Apps
text:
Shiny is an R package that allows analysts and marketers to build interactive web applications directly from R. Unlike static dashboards, Shiny apps let users explore data dynamically through filters, inputs, and real‑time updates.


A Shiny app has two main components:


Defines what the user sees:

Dropdown menus

Sliders

Plots

Tables

Text outputs




Defines how the app responds:

Reads user inputs

Runs R code dynamically

Updates visualizations in real time

Together, the UI and server create an interactive experience without requiring JavaScript or web development skills.

title: Essay on Shiny Apps
text:

Defines what the user sees:

Dropdown menus

Tables

Text outputs




Defines how the app responds:

Runs R code dynamically

Updates visualizations in real time

Together, the UI and server create an interactive experience without requiring JavaScript or web development skills.

title: Essay on Shiny Apps
text:
Summary
Shiny apps are ideal when: - You want interactivity - Your audience needs to explore data - You want to build tools without learning JavaScript
They are a key skill for modern data‑driven marketing teams.

title: Dashboard
text:
The dashboard highlights:

Key performance metrics

Trends and comparisons

Visual summaries designed for non‑technical audiences

Insights that support data‑driven decision‑making

title: Dashboard
text:
Dashboard Code & Output
Below is the embedded dashboard from my CEP project.

title: Dashboard
text:
Creating this dashboard helped me practice: - Structuring insights for clarity - Designing visuals that support storytelling - Communicating findings to a non‑technical audience
This aligns with my career goal of working in integrated marketing within sports and entertainment, where clear communication and data‑driven insights are essential.

title: Essay on Shinylive
text:
Shinylive is a new way to run Shiny applications directly in the browser, without a server.
This is possible because R code is compiled to WebAssembly and executed client‑side.
In other words:

Traditional Shiny = requires an R server

Shinylive = runs entirely in the user’s browser

This makes Shiny apps easier to share, embed, and publish.

title: Essay on Shinylive
text:
✔️ Pros of Shinylive

No server required

Free hosting

Easy to share

Great for teaching, demos, and lightweight apps

title: Essay on Shinylive
text:
❗ Cons of Shinylive

Not ideal for large datasets

Browser memory limits

Slower for heavy computations

Fewer UI customization options

title: Dashboard
text:
This page showcases the static dashboard I created in Module W10 using Quarto.
The goal of this dashboard is to communicate insights from my MSDM CEP project using clear visualizations and audience‑friendly storytelling.

title: Dashboard
text:
📊 Trust Item Breakdown

trust_items_long <- trust_clean %>%
  select(condition, starts_with("trust_")) %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = starts_with("trust_"), names_to = "item", values_to = "score")

ggplot(trust_items_long, aes(x = item, y = score, fill = condition)) +
  stat_summary(fun = "mean", geom = "col", position = "dodge") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title = "Trust Item Breakdown by Condition",
    subtitle = "Average score for each trust dimension",
    x = "Trust Item",
    y = "Average Score (1–5)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14)

Warning: Removed 23 rows containing non-finite outside the scale range
(`stat_summary()`).















Tip



Insight Across all 10 trust dimensions — including authenticity, transparency, and data comfort — the AI‑personalized email consistently performs as well as or better than the control email.

title: Dashboard
section: 📝 Executive Summary - What This Means for MEin3D
text:
📝 Executive Summary - What This Means for MEin3D

The dashboard shows that AI‑generated email content performs on par with human‑written messaging in terms of trust. Customers rate both emails as similarly trustworthy, meaning MEin3D can safely adopt AI‑powered personalization without risking customer confidence. This supports shifting toward automated, scalable email workflows that reduce production time while maintaining strong customer perception.

title: Dashboard
text:
📊 Trust Item for Second Visualization

trust_items_long <- trust_clean %>%
  select(condition, starts_with("trust_")) %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = starts_with("trust_"), names_to = "item", values_to = "score") %>%
  left_join(trust_labels, by = "item")

objectID: W10 Dashboards Assignment — MEin3D Trust Insights.html
href: W10 Dashboards Assignment — MEin3D Trust Insights.html
title: W10 Dashboards Assignment — MEin3D Trust Insights
text:
The goal of this project was to create a static dashboard using Quarto that communicates insights from my MEin3D Culminating Experience Project. I focused on understanding how consumers perceive trust in AI‑generated versus human‑written email content. The dashboard presents a clear narrative supported by visualizations that summarize trust scores across ten Likert‑scale items.



The dataset used for this dashboard is the MEin3D Trust Survey, which includes 130 responses. Participants were randomly assigned to one of two conditions:

AI‑personalized email
Control (human‑written) email

Each participant rated ten trust‑related statements on a 1–5 Likert scale.
A composite trust score was also calculated.



Below is the full code used to generate the static dashboard.
This code is shown in an unexecuted chunk, as required.


Code
# Load cleaned trust dataset
library(tidyverse)

trust_clean <- read_csv("~/Desktop/MEin3D CEP Project/MEin3D_TrustSurvey_CLEAN.csv")

# Lookup table: trust item → full question text
trust_labels <- tibble(
  item = c("trust_1","trust_2","trust_3","trust_4","trust_5",
           "trust_6","trust_7","trust_8","trust_9","trust_10"),
  question = c(
    "I trust this brand.",
    "This email feels genuine.",
    "This email shows that the brand understands my needs.",
    "The information in this email feels accurate and reliable.",
    "I feel comfortable with how this brand uses my data in this email.",
    "This email is transparent about why I am receiving it.",
    "This email makes me feel valued as a customer.",
    "I believe this brand uses my information responsibly.",
    "This email increases my confidence in this brand.",
    "I would be willing to receive more emails like this from the brand."
  )
)

# Summaries for visualization
trust_summary <- trust_clean %>%
  group_by(condition) %>%
  summarize(avg_trust = mean(trust_score_avg, na.rm = TRUE))

# Prepare trust items for second visualization
trust_items_long <- trust_clean %>%
  select(condition, starts_with("trust_")) %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = starts_with("trust_"), names_to = "item", values_to = "score") %>%
  left_join(trust_labels, by = "item")

# Plotting code omitted for brevity in this report





The static dashboard revealed several key insights: AI‑personalized emails produced slightly higher average trust scores than the control email. Across all ten trust dimensions, AI‑generated content performed as well as or better than human‑written content. Participants consistently rated the AI email as transparent, accurate, and respectful of their data.



The results suggest that consumers are comfortable with AI‑generated email content and do not perceive it as less trustworthy than human‑written messaging. This supports MEin3D’s strategy to scale personalized communication using AI without risking customer trust.



https://klee597.github.io/M10/

title: W10 Dashboards Assignment — MEin3D Trust Insights
text:
Data Description
The same MEin3D Trust Survey dataset was used. The interactive app reshapes the data into long format and joins each trust item with its corresponding question text for improved readability.

title: W10 Dashboards Assignment — MEin3D Trust Insights
section: Key Findings from the Interactive App
text:
Key Findings from the Interactive App
The Shiny app provides deeper insight into trust dynamics: Users can compare trust items individually rather than relying on a single composite score. The Distribution Explorer shows that trust scores are consistently high across both conditions. The Summary Table highlights subtle differences between AI and control emails, with AI often performing slightly better.

title: W10 Dashboards Assignment — MEin3D Trust Insights
text:
Interpretation of the Interactive Dashboard
The interactive tool reinforces the conclusion that AI‑generated email content is perceived as trustworthy and reliable. It also allows stakeholders to explore specific trust dimensions that matter most to MEin3D’s communication strategy.

The goal of this project was to create a static dashboard using Quarto that communicates insights from my MEin3D Culminating Experience Project. Code
# Load cleaned trust dataset
library(tidyverse)

trust_clean <- read_csv("~/Desktop/MEin3D CEP Project/MEin3D_TrustSurvey_CLEAN.csv")

trust_labels <- tibble(

    "This email is transparent about why I am receiving it.",
    "This email makes me feel valued as a customer.",
    "I believe this brand uses my information responsibly.",
    "This email increases my confidence in this brand.",
